research_topic: Canvas Learning System frontend tech stack and learning algorithm academic validation

domains.title: BKT + FSRS Dual-Engine Mastery Tracking
domains.relevance: Validates DE-3 backend algorithm combining BKT knowledge tracing with FSRS spaced repetition

papers.title: Investigating the Robustness of Knowledge Tracing Models in the Presence of Student Concept Drift
papers.key_finding: BKT is the most reliably robust KT model across all three recorded metrics when applied to student data spanning multiple academic years. More complex attention-based models lose predictive power significantly faster under concept drift. This validates using BKT as a stable baseline in a dual-engine approach.
papers.relevance_to_project: STRONG - Directly supports using BKT as stable mastery backbone. BKT's robustness under temporal drift means it provides reliable long-term mastery estimates even as learner populations shift.

papers.title: Optimizing Spaced Repetition Schedule by Capturing the Dynamics of Memory
papers.authors: Su, J., Ye, J., Nie, L., Cao, Y., Chen, Y.
papers.venue: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
papers.key_finding: Proposes the DSR (Difficulty, Stability, Retrievability) memory model with Markov property, transforming spaced repetition into a stochastic shortest path problem. FSRS is the open-source implementation of this approach. Reduces 64% error and 17% cost vs baselines in recall rate prediction and schedule optimization.
papers.relevance_to_project: STRONG - This is the foundational paper for FSRS, the exact algorithm used in Canvas Learning System. The DSR model provides the mathematical basis for the spaced repetition engine.

papers.title: A Stochastic Shortest Path Algorithm for Optimizing Spaced Repetition Scheduling
papers.authors: Ye, J., Su, J., Cao, Y.
papers.key_finding: SSP-MMC algorithm trained on 220M memory behavior logs. Establishes the stochastic shortest path framework that FSRS builds upon. Demonstrates that ML-based scheduling significantly outperforms hand-tuned algorithms like SM-2.
papers.relevance_to_project: STRONG - Conference paper establishing SSP-MMC, the precursor to FSRS. Validates the core algorithmic approach with massive real-world data.

papers.title: LECTOR: LLM-Enhanced Concept-based Test-Oriented Repetition for Adaptive Spaced Learning
papers.key_finding: Integrates LLM-based semantic analysis with adaptive interval optimization and personalized learning profiles. LECTOR achieves 90.2% success rate vs FSRS 89.6% in test-oriented scenarios. Demonstrates that combining knowledge tracing with spaced repetition produces superior results.
papers.relevance_to_project: MODERATE - Validates the dual-engine concept (KT + SR). Shows that augmenting FSRS with semantic/concept understanding (which BKT provides in our system) yields measurable improvements.

papers.title: Survey of Loss Augmented Knowledge Tracing
papers.key_finding: Comprehensive survey covering BKT limitations (binary state, no long-term dependencies) and DKT improvements. Identifies contrastive learning and loss regularization as key advancement techniques. BKT remains interpretable and useful for concept-level tracking despite limitations.
papers.relevance_to_project: MODERATE - Confirms BKT's role as interpretable concept-level tracker. Supports our architecture where BKT handles per-concept mastery estimation while FSRS handles optimal review scheduling.

domains.synthesis: The dual BKT+FSRS engine is well-supported by recent research. BKT's robustness under concept drift (2025, arXiv:2511.00704) makes it ideal for stable mastery estimation, while FSRS's SSP-MMC mathematical foundation (KDD 2022, IEEE TKDE 2023) provides state-of-the-art review scheduling. LECTOR (2025) further validates that combining knowledge tracing with spaced repetition is a fruitful direction. The architecture of using BKT for mastery state and FSRS for scheduling is academically sound.

domains.title: Interactive Knowledge Graphs in Adaptive Learning
domains.relevance: Validates ReactFlow/canvas whiteboard for educational knowledge visualization

papers.title: GraphRAG-Induced Dual Knowledge Structure Graphs for Personalized Learning Path Recommendation
papers.key_finding: EDU-GraphRAG adaptively constructs knowledge concept structure graphs capturing prerequisite and similarity relationships. Uses TextGrad to reduce LLM hallucinations. Demonstrates that graph-based knowledge structures significantly improve learning path recommendation interpretability and learner trust.
papers.relevance_to_project: STRONG - Directly validates our canvas-based knowledge graph approach. The prerequisite/similarity relationship model maps to our canvas edge types. Graph visualization of learning paths is shown to improve learner trust and understanding.

papers.title: Leveraging Graph Retrieval-Augmented Generation to Support Learners' Understanding of Knowledge Concepts in MOOCs
papers.key_finding: Graph RAG applied to educational knowledge concepts in MOOCs. Demonstrates that graph-structured knowledge representation improves learner understanding compared to flat content presentation.
papers.relevance_to_project: MODERATE - Supports using graph-based visualization for knowledge concept navigation, which is exactly what Canvas Learning System's whiteboard provides.

papers.title: A Systematic Literature Review of Knowledge Graph Construction and Application in Education
papers.key_finding: Comprehensive review identifying 5 primary KG applications in education: Adaptive/Personalized Learning, Curriculum Design, Concept Mapping/Visualization, Semantic Search, and Content Recommendation. Cognitive graphs integrating KG with cognitive reasoning visualize students' cognitive maps. KGs are 'indispensable tools' for enriching personalized learning.
papers.relevance_to_project: STRONG - Validates the entire premise of using knowledge graphs for educational content. The 5 identified application domains map directly to Canvas Learning System features.

papers.title: Utilization of Knowledge Graphs Created with Generative AI and Visualization Techniques to Enhance Understanding of Computer Science Concepts
papers.key_finding: LLM-generated knowledge graphs with interactive visualization significantly enhance concept understanding in undergraduate CS education. Demonstrates that visual graph interfaces improve learning outcomes compared to traditional linear presentation.
papers.relevance_to_project: STRONG - Directly validates our approach of AI-assisted knowledge graph construction combined with interactive visualization. The ReactFlow/canvas paradigm aligns with this research direction.

papers.title: ACE: AI-Assisted Construction of Educational Knowledge Graphs with Prerequisite Relations
papers.key_finding: AI-assisted pipeline for constructing educational knowledge graphs with prerequisite relations. Demonstrates practical methodology for building the type of knowledge structures our system visualizes.
papers.relevance_to_project: MODERATE - Provides methodology validation for AI-assisted KG construction that feeds into our canvas visualization.

papers.title: Personalized Learning Path Recommendation Based on Knowledge Graphs: A Survey
papers.key_finding: Comprehensive survey of KG-based learning path recommendation with emphasis on Bloom's taxonomy alignment. Graph Attention Networks and deep reinforcement learning show promise for personalized path recommendation through knowledge graphs.
papers.relevance_to_project: MODERATE - Validates that knowledge graph-based learning path recommendation is an active, well-researched direction. Our canvas edge-based path visualization aligns with this research.

domains.synthesis: Interactive knowledge graphs for education are extensively validated by recent research. The Heliyon 2024 systematic review identifies KGs as 'indispensable' for adaptive learning. EDU-GraphRAG (2025) specifically validates using graph structures with prerequisite/similarity edges for learning path recommendation - exactly our canvas model. The Springer 2026 paper confirms that AI-generated KGs with interactive visualization enhance concept understanding. ReactFlow/canvas as the UI layer for these knowledge graphs is architecturally sound.

domains.title: Stealth Assessment / Implicit Evaluation in AI-Assisted Learning
domains.relevance: Validates unobtrusive mastery assessment through learning behavior analysis

papers.title: Stealth Assessment: A Theoretically Grounded and Psychometrically Sound Method to Assess, Support, and Investigate Learning in Technology-Rich Environments
papers.key_finding: Comprehensive framework establishing stealth assessment as theoretically grounded and psychometrically valid. Based on Evidence-Centered Design (ECD). Enables continuous, unobtrusive assessment of competencies including creativity, persistence, problem-solving, and computational thinking without disrupting learning flow.
papers.relevance_to_project: STRONG - Directly validates our approach of assessing mastery through learning behavior rather than explicit testing. ECD framework provides the theoretical basis for inferring mastery from canvas interactions.

papers.title: A Scalable, Flexible, and Interpretable Analytic Pipeline for Stealth Assessment in Complex Digital Game-Based Learning Environments
papers.authors: Lu, W., Laffey, J., Sadler, T., Griffin, J., Goggins, S.
papers.key_finding: Proposes a scalable stealth assessment pipeline using educational data mining for generalizability across DGBL contexts. Pipeline: ECD framework -> feature generation -> model selection -> preprocessing -> evaluation -> inference. Demonstrates practical implementation of stealth assessment at scale.
papers.relevance_to_project: STRONG - Provides a concrete implementation pipeline for stealth assessment. The feature generation and model inference stages map to our behavior tracking and mastery estimation components.

papers.title: Stealth Assessments in Digital Learning Environments: Current Trends, New Directions, and Ethical Considerations
papers.venue: Journal of Research on Technology in Education (Special Issue)
papers.key_finding: Special issue with 11 papers covering current trends in stealth assessment. Addresses ethical considerations of unobtrusive assessment. Establishes stealth assessment as a mainstream approach in digital learning environments.
papers.relevance_to_project: MODERATE - Validates stealth assessment as mainstream and growing research area. Ethical considerations section relevant to our implementation.

papers.title: Enhancing Stealth Assessment in Game-Based Learning Through Goal Recognition
papers.venue: Journal of Research on Technology in Education
papers.key_finding: Goal recognition significantly improves concept-level assessment accuracy in stealth assessment. Demonstrates that understanding learner intent (goals) enhances the accuracy of competency inference.
papers.relevance_to_project: MODERATE - Goal recognition parallels our approach of inferring learning intent from canvas navigation patterns. Validates that behavioral pattern analysis can improve assessment accuracy.

papers.title: Game-Based Learning Prediction Model Construction: Towards Validated Stealth Assessment Implementation
papers.key_finding: Systematically implements stealth assessment using ECD framework for Mission HydroSci. Adaptive stealth assessment provides reliable information about learning growth. Demonstrates that stealth assessment can capture learning progression over time.
papers.relevance_to_project: MODERATE - Validates that stealth assessment reliably captures learning growth, which is the core function of our mastery tracking system.

papers.title: AI-Driven Formative Assessment and Adaptive Learning in Data-Science Education
papers.key_finding: VITA (Virtual Teaching Assistant) automates formative assessments using AI. Demonstrates that AI-driven assessment integrated into learning workflows supports large-scale personalized education. LLM-powered assessment provides timely, personalized feedback.
papers.relevance_to_project: MODERATE - Validates AI-driven formative assessment integration. Our system's AI chat + stealth assessment combination aligns with this direction.

domains.synthesis: Stealth assessment is well-established and actively growing. Shute & Rahimi (2024) provide the definitive theoretical framework via ECD. The JEDM 2024 pipeline paper demonstrates practical scalability. A dedicated JRTE special issue (2025) with 11 papers confirms mainstream adoption. Our approach of inferring mastery from canvas interaction behaviors (navigation patterns, time-on-concept, review frequency) is directly supported by this literature. The key insight is ECD: define competency model -> identify observable evidence -> design tasks that produce evidence.

domains.title: Local-First Application Architecture
domains.relevance: Validates Obsidian-based local-first architecture with optional cloud sync

papers.title: Local-First Software: You Own Your Data, in Spite of the Cloud
papers.authors: Kleppmann, M., Wiggins, A., van Hardenberg, P., McGranaghan, M.
papers.key_finding: Foundational paper establishing 7 ideals for local-first software: fast, multi-device, offline, collaboration, longevity, privacy, user control. CRDTs identified as key enabling technology. Demonstrated by apps like Linear, Figma, Notion.
papers.relevance_to_project: STRONG - Foundational justification for our Obsidian-based architecture. All 7 local-first ideals align with our design: local vault storage, offline operation, user data ownership.

papers.title: Consistent Local-First Software: Enforcing Safety and Invariants for Local-First Applications
papers.authors: Kohler, M., Zakhour, G., Weisenburger, P., Salvaneschi, G.
papers.key_finding: ConLoc system automatically verifies which methods require strong consistency vs weak consistency in local-first apps. Demonstrates that local-first applications can enforce safety invariants while maintaining offline capability. Published in top SE journal.
papers.relevance_to_project: STRONG - Addresses a key concern: maintaining data consistency in our local-first architecture. ConLoc's approach of selectively applying strong/weak consistency maps to our sync engine design.

papers.title: The Art of the Fugue: Minimizing Interleaving in Collaborative Text Editing
papers.venue: IEEE Transactions on Parallel and Distributed Systems, 36(11), 2425-2437
papers.key_finding: Advances CRDT-based collaborative editing with reduced interleaving artifacts. Demonstrates continued active research in making local-first collaboration practical and high-quality.
papers.relevance_to_project: LOW - Tangential but confirms active CRDT research. Our system's collaborative features may benefit from these advances.

papers.title: Optimizing CRDTs for Low Memory Environments
papers.key_finding: CRDTs optimized for resource-constrained environments. Demonstrates that local-first data structures can work efficiently even on limited hardware.
papers.relevance_to_project: LOW - Validates that CRDT/local-first approaches are being optimized for broader deployment, including desktop applications.

domains.synthesis: Local-first architecture is academically well-established since Kleppmann et al. (2019) and actively advancing. The IEEE TSE 2025 paper on ConLoc addresses the key challenge of maintaining invariants in local-first apps. Our Obsidian-based architecture naturally inherits local-first properties: data lives in the user's vault as markdown/JSON files, works offline, and syncs optionally. This is not just a convenience choice but an architecturally sound approach with strong academic backing.

domains.title: Desktop-Native AI Tools in Education
domains.relevance: Validates desktop (Obsidian/Electron) over browser-based approach for AI learning tools

papers.title: Data Privacy in AI-Driven Education: An In-Depth Exploration Into Data Privacy Concerns and Potential Solutions
papers.key_finding: AI-integrated educational platforms collect massive amounts of personal student information, creating data protection challenges. Local processing and data sovereignty are identified as key solutions. Desktop-native architectures that keep data local are recommended over cloud-dependent approaches.
papers.relevance_to_project: STRONG - Directly supports our desktop-native, local-first approach. Student data privacy concerns are best addressed when data never leaves the device.

papers.title: Building Privacy and Preserving AI Models for Secure Student Data Management in Educational Technology Platforms
papers.key_finding: Proposes framework integrating Federated Learning with Differential Privacy where student data remains on devices. Only encrypted model updates are transmitted. Demonstrates that privacy-preserving AI in education is technically feasible with local-first architectures.
papers.relevance_to_project: STRONG - Validates our architecture where learning data stays in Obsidian vault. If we add cloud features, federated learning approach provides a privacy-preserving path.

papers.title: Student-Centric Ethical Frameworks for AI-Driven Education: Participatory Consent and Data Ownership
papers.key_finding: Emphasizes participatory consent and data ownership in AI education. Students should own their learning data. Local-first desktop applications naturally satisfy data ownership requirements.
papers.relevance_to_project: MODERATE - Validates the ethical dimension of our local-first choice. Student data ownership is a core value proposition.

papers.title: Towards Responsible Artificial Intelligence in Education: A Systematic Review on Identifying and Mitigating Ethical Risks
papers.key_finding: Systematic review identifying ethical risks in AI education including data privacy, surveillance, and bias. Recommends local processing, transparency, and user control as mitigation strategies.
papers.relevance_to_project: MODERATE - Validates our ethical approach. Desktop-native + local-first + transparent AI = responsible AI in education.

papers.title: The Impact of AI-Based Learning Tools in Academic Innovation: A Review of DeepSeek, GPT, and Gemini (2020-2025)
papers.key_finding: Comprehensive review of AI learning tool effectiveness. 57.9% of researchers prefer desktop tools for stability and larger screen. AI tools demonstrably improve learning outcomes when used appropriately. Desktop environments provide more focused learning experiences.
papers.relevance_to_project: MODERATE - Validates desktop preference among academic users. Stability and focus advantages of desktop tools support our Obsidian-based approach over web-based alternatives.

papers.title: Analyzing Security and Privacy Challenges in Generative AI Usage Guidelines for Higher Education
papers.key_finding: Significant gaps exist in institutional AI privacy policies. Desktop-native tools with local data processing sidestep many institutional compliance challenges that cloud-based AI tools face.
papers.relevance_to_project: MODERATE - Validates that local-first desktop approach simplifies compliance and reduces privacy risks compared to cloud-based alternatives.

domains.synthesis: While no single paper directly studies 'desktop-native vs browser AI for education', the convergent evidence is strong: (1) AI education tools collect sensitive data that should stay local (multiple 2024-2025 papers); (2) Federated learning/local processing is the recommended privacy solution; (3) 57.9% of researchers prefer desktop for stability; (4) GDPR/FERPA compliance is simpler with local data. Our Obsidian/Electron desktop-native approach is validated by the intersection of privacy research, educational AI effectiveness research, and local-first architecture research.
domains.caveats: No direct comparative study of desktop-native vs browser-based AI learning tools was found. Validation is inferred from convergent evidence across privacy, local-first, and educational AI research.

key_findings: BKT+FSRS dual engine: BKT's robustness under concept drift (2025) + FSRS's SSP-MMC mathematical foundation (KDD 2022, IEEE TKDE 2023) = academically sound combination
Knowledge graph for education: Systematic reviews (2024) confirm KGs are 'indispensable' for adaptive learning. EDU-GraphRAG (2025) validates graph-based learning path recommendation
Stealth assessment: Shute & Rahimi (2024) definitive ECD framework + JEDM scalable pipeline + JRTE 2025 special issue = mature, mainstream approach
Local-first architecture: Kleppmann et al. (2019) foundational + IEEE TSE 2025 ConLoc consistency guarantees = academically rigorous
Desktop-native AI education: Convergent evidence from privacy, compliance, and user preference research supports desktop-local approach, though no direct comparative study exists
gaps_identified: No paper directly combines BKT+FSRS in a single system - this is our novel contribution
No study of ReactFlow specifically for educational knowledge graphs - ReactFlow is validated as a general-purpose graph visualization tool
Desktop-native vs browser AI learning tool comparison study does not exist in literature
Stealth assessment research is primarily in game-based learning; application to knowledge graph/canvas environments is less studied
